cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60-year-old attends ED with with respiratory distress and tachycardic. Associated features include with pneumonia and febrile.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with advanced CKD presents with reduced appetite and progressive fatigue over several days.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~30.5 (+/-2); measured 30.7 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 137 - (101 + 15) = 21, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with longstanding breathlessness presents with increased sputum and fatigue.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~33.6; measured 33.5 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 39-year-old presents with reduced consciousness and marked hypoventilation after multiple sedating agents.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.3; measured 26.5 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 9.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.8; measured 27.1 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 11.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 54-year-old becomes sleepy with shallow breathing after a sedating treatment.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~25.1; measured 24.8 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 9.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~25.9; measured 26.3 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 12.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 32-year-old is brought in drowsy with bradypnoea and decreased responsiveness.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~25.8; measured 25.0 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 29-year-old presents with light-headedness and dehydration after several days of gastroenteritis symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~36.5 (+/-2); measured 37.6 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 141 - (112 + 19) = 10, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 23-year-old comes to ED after several days of illness, with recent weight loss and reduced oral intake.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~30.5 (+/-2); measured 30.6 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 138 - (99 + 15) = 24 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~49.8; measured 48.6 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~26.0 (+/-2); measured 24.8 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 141 - (118 + 12) = 11, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents confused, tachypnoeic, and febrile with ringing in the ears.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (25.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~34.0; measured 34.1 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old presents dehydrated and weak after several days of persistent upper gastrointestinal losses.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~49.8; measured 49.3 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 22-year-old presents with sudden shortness of breath, tingling fingers, and chest tightness during emotional distress.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.4; measured 21.8 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: Simple respiratory alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old presents with fast breathing, hand tingling, and escalating distress without focal findings.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. For acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.2; measured 21.2 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 8.0. This pattern is consistent with simple hyperventilation.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60-year-old presents to ED with tachycardic, febrile, with suspected sepsis, and tachypnoeic.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents to ED with nausea and abdominal discomfort, marked thirst, and tachypnoea.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~30.5 (+/-2); measured 30.5 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 136 - (97 + 15) = 24 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 52-year-old is difficult to rouse with shallow respirations after multiple sedating medications.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.4; measured 27.2 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 11.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 21-year-old presents with tinnitus, vomiting, fever, and rapid breathing after an overdose.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (26.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 79-year-old on regular frusemide presents with light-headedness and recent poor oral intake after a small increase in diuretic use.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~44.9; measured 43.6 is appropriate. Anion gap is 141 - (100 + 31) = 10, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~32.4; measured 32.1 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 69-year-old taking loop diuretics reports postural dizziness and fatigue after several days of reduced intake.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: The pH is near normal, but the elevated HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis with compensation. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~44.2; measured 44.2 is appropriate. Anion gap is 137 - (98 + 30) = 9, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 68-year-old with advanced kidney disease presents drowsy with nausea, reduced urine output, and progressive weakness.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~26.0 (+/-2); measured 24.5 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 132 - (94 + 12) = 26, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.
